1:
The witnesses open the reign of Ahaz with a chronological note and a moral verdict, and the two grammatical-historical voices are chiefly occupied with the dates. John Gill reconciles the synchronism: Jotham began in Pekah's second year and reigned sixteen years, so his last would fall in Pekah's eighteenth; yet since his first year opened at the beginning of Pekah's second, his close came toward the end of the seventeenth, exactly as the verse reports. Keil and Delitzsch lift the eye from the arithmetic to the significance: with Ahaz "a most eventful change" overtook Judah. Under the vigorous reigns of Uzziah and Jotham the kingdom had prospered, but — as the early chapters of Isaiah show (Isaiah 2–6) — prosperity had bred luxury, self-security, and forgetfulness of God among the upper classes. Under Ahaz these sins hardened into open apostasy: image-worship, idolatry of every kind, and at last the shutting of the temple doors, punished at once by the crushing Syro-Israelite defeat.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the reign its epitaph: "Few and evil were his days" — few, for he died at thirty-six; evil, for he had none of David's affection for the instituted worship of God. Henry counts the aggravations. It was Ahaz's honour to be of the house and lineage of David, and it was to God's ancient covenant with David that he owed his throne, so that his wickedness was a reproach to that honourable name — degeneranti genus opprobrium, a good extraction is a disgrace to him who degenerates from it. He walked in the way of the kings of Israel with no reason of state to plead as they had; he made his sons pass through the fire to his "dunghill-deities"; and he sacrificed in the high places, forsaking God's house for the green hills, "a religion little worth, which was guided by fancy, not by faith."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke, surveying the chapter as a whole, lays out the road Ahaz will travel: he restores idolatry, is besieged by Rezin and Pekah but not taken, hires Tiglath-pileser with the silver and gold of the Lord's house, copies a Damascus altar, and strips the temple's furniture before dying and leaving the throne to Hezekiah. The voices do not divide here; together they set the frame — a king of David's line who, uniquely without even the political pretext of the northern kings, chose their idolatry, so that (as Henry adds) those whose hearts condemn them will go anywhere in a day of distress rather than to God.
2:
Here the witnesses meet the chapter's first real crux — the age of Ahaz — and they divide over how to solve it. The verse says he was twenty when he began to reign and reigned sixteen years, dying therefore at thirty-six; but Hezekiah his son was twenty-five at his own accession (2 Kings 18:2), which would make Ahaz a father at eleven. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the bolder course and emends the text: the number should probably be twenty-five, following the LXX, Syriac, and Arabic of the parallel (2 Chronicles 28:1), for otherwise Ahaz was begotten when his own father was but ten or eleven — a thing perhaps not impossible in the East, but hardly likely.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke reviews the same difficulty and reaches the opposite verdict. He notices the alternative proposal — that the twenty years count from the beginning of Jotham's reign, making Ahaz fifty-two at death — but rejects it as "a violent solution, worthy of little credit." Better, he says, to return to the text as it stands and allow that Ahaz might have been only eleven or twelve when Hezekiah was born; for in eastern countries the young of both sexes are marriageable at ten or twelve and are often betrothed at nine, and he cites a woman of his own acquaintance who bore her first child between eleven and twelve. He holds it "quite a possible case."

The remaining voices pass over the puzzle to the substance. The reformed John Gill notes only that Ahaz reigned the same number of years as his father Jotham, sixteen. Keil and Delitzsch fix the theological weight of the phrase "he walked in the way of the kings of Israel," which the Chronicler explains by adding that he "made also molten images for the Baals" (2 Chronicles 28:2). That "way," they observe, means first the calf-worship in which all the kings of Israel walked; but — since the same formula is used of Joram of Judah in 2 Kings 8:18 — it here embraces the worship of Baal as well. The single point on which all agree is the tragedy the numbers frame: measured against David his father, Ahaz "did not that which was right in the sight of the LORD his God."
3:
The verse doubles its indictment — Ahaz walked in Israel's way and "made his son to pass through the fire" — and the witnesses concentrate on the second, more dreadful clause. The great question is how literally to read it, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is emphatic: the Chronicler "rightly explains this as a sacrifice by fire," and the appalling rite is really intended, as a chain of prophets attests (2 Kings 17:31; Jeremiah 19:5; Ezekiel 16:20; Jeremiah 32:35). To "make to pass through the fire to Molech" (Leviticus 18:21) may have arisen, he grants, from the notion that burning was a passage to union with the deity once the dross of flesh was purged, or it may be a mere euphemism; but Ahaz was, he thinks, the first Israelite king to offer such a sacrifice, and he offered it as "a last desperate resource" against his northern enemies — a deed of dire extremity, not, as some absurdly suppose, of love to his child (compare 2 Kings 3:27). The heathen he imitated were "more particularly the Ammonites."

The wesleyan voices agree on the horror without pausing over lexical hesitation. John Wesley glosses "pass" bluntly: it was "by way of oblation, so as to be consumed for a burnt-offering," the practice of the heathen and of the Israelites who aped them. Adam Clarke, characteristically, refers the reader to his fuller notes on the Levitical laws against giving one's seed to Molech (Leviticus 20:2), where the whole subject is weighed at large.

The reformed John Gill turns to the first clause and draws out its peculiar folly. Ahaz walked in the way of the kings of Israel, "worshipping the calves as they did"; but where those kings had at least a political motive — the fear that pilgrimage to Jerusalem would draw their people back to the house of David — Ahaz had none. Their idolatry, corrupt as it was, served their interest of state; his was "contrary to the religious sentiments in which he was educated" and against his own advantage besides. The witnesses do not contend; together they present a king who chose the cruelest rite of the surrounding nations, without even the wretched excuse of policy that had first bred the sin in the north — an idolatry so gratuitous that it condemns itself.
4:
The catalogue of Ahaz's apostasy closes with his worship "in the high places, and on the hills, and under every green tree," and the witnesses read the verse for both its topography and its heart. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the close philology. He notices that "the high places" and "the hills" are marked off by two different prepositions in the Hebrew as in the English, and are not to be confused: a bâmâh, or "high-place," was a local sanctuary, and even a sacred pillar or altar might bear the name — Mesha king of Moab, he recalls, speaks of his own pillar as "this bâmath." The phrase "under every green tree" he sets beside its prophetic parallels (1 Kings 14:23; Hosea 14:8), and, following Thenius, glosses it not so much a green as a thick-foliaged, shadow-yielding tree — for, as Hosea complains, they "burn incense under oaks and poplars and terebinths, because the shadow thereof is good" (Hosea 4:13).

The reformed John Gill weighs the enormity by comparison. This, he insists, "none of the kings of Judah before him ever did"; earlier kings had connived at the people's worship on the high places, but "they never encouraged it by their own example." And he presses a sharp distinction between the people and their king: where the multitude sacrificed to the true God, though at a wrong place, Ahaz "very probably offered sacrifices there to idols" — a corruption not merely of place but of object, worse in the sovereign than in the crowd he led.

Keil and Delitzsch, reading straight on, treat the verse as the hinge of the chapter: the record of private devotion at every hill and tree gives way at once to the public catastrophe, as "Rezin king of Syria and Pekah son of Remaliah" march up to Jerusalem. The witnesses do not divide here; together they mark the completeness of the ruin — a Davidic king who forsook the one appointed altar for the shade of every hilltop grove, and whose fancy-led religion, guided by the eye rather than by faith, left him defenceless when the northern armies came. What begins as a note on where Ahaz worshipped becomes, in their reading, the measure of how far he had wandered from the God who had chosen one place for his name.
5:
With the Syro-Ephraimite war the narrative turns from Ahaz's sin to his peril, and the witnesses read the siege as at once a chastisement and a deliverance. The grammatical-historical commentators anchor it in Isaiah. Charles Ellicott notes that the verse "agrees almost word for word" with the prophet's own account (Isaiah 7:1), and, quoting Robertson Smith, sets the scene: Jotham had died "at a critical moment," Pekah and Rezin suspended their feuds to make common cause against Judah, and the allies meant to dethrone the house of David and set up a creature of their own, "the son of Tabeal" (Isaiah 7:6). Yet they "could not overcome him" — literally, they "were not able to war," for the city had been strongly fortified by Uzziah and Jotham (2 Chronicles 26:9; 2 Chronicles 27:3). Adam Clarke adds that this was likely the moment Isaiah was sent to console the trembling king and to predict the death of both his enemies (Isaiah 7:1).

The reason the siege failed is, for the others, wholly theological. The wesleyan John Wesley states it flatly: the allies "could not overcome" because "God of his own mere grace undertook his protection, and disappointed the hopes of his enemies." The reformed John Gill sees the same providence from the other side: Rezin and Pekah "came up to Jerusalem to war," but they were "moved to it by the Lord, to chastise Ahaz for his idolatry" — the very enemies who threaten him are the instruments of his discipline.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the moral the whole chapter will illustrate. The confederates aimed to seize Jerusalem and enthrone a king of their own (Isaiah 7:6) and fell short of it; yet "what can those keep that have lost their religion?" Having forsaken God, Ahaz "had neither courage nor strength to make head against his enemies, nor could he, with any boldness, ask help of God." His recourse — the appeal to Assyria that follows — Henry reads as apostasy compounding apostasy: "Was it because there was not a God in Israel that he sent to the Assyrian for help?" The witnesses agree: the man who would not turn to the God who was even now sparing him turned instead to a pagan king, so that the very deliverance became the occasion of a deeper bondage.
6:
A single place-name — Elath, the port at the head of the Red Sea — draws the witnesses into a textual debate, and the grammatical-historical voices carry it. Charles Ellicott treats the verse as a parenthesis, marking the epoch at which Judah lost "its only harbour and chief emporium." He then presses the crux: the words for Syria and Edom, Syrians and Edomites, are nearly identical in Hebrew, and the Hebrew margin, many manuscripts, the LXX, and the Vulgate all read "Edomites" for "Syrians" here. If that is right, we must also restore "Edom" for "Syria," and the sense becomes that Rezin "emancipated the Edomites from the yoke of Judah" which Uzziah had imposed (2 Kings 14:22), so winning their help against Ahaz. Yet Ellicott records that Bähr prefers the received text, supposing Rezin simply expelled the Jews and planted a Syrian colony in their place.

The reformed John Gill has already weighed the same variant and reached a cautious verdict. He rehearses Elath's tangled history — a port once Edom's, taken by David, lost when Edom revolted under Joram, apparently retaken by Amaziah, and rebuilt by Azariah to Judah — and concludes that "it seems by this that it had been in the hands of the Syrians, who now recovered it." He notes that Le Clerc ventured to substitute "Edom" for "Aram" but did so "without any authority," and Gill declines to follow him. Adam Clarke simply refers the reader back to Elath's earlier recovery under Azariah (2 Kings 14:22).

Beneath the dispute over a single letter, the witnesses agree on the loss the verse records and its place in the story. Whether Rezin freed a subject Edom or displaced the men of Judah with Syrians, the effect was the same: while the siege of Jerusalem failed, the war stripped Ahaz of his southern harbour and its trade, "a grave blow to the national prosperity." The reader is thus prepared, in Ellicott's reading, for what comes next — the despairing embassy to Assyria of v.7 — since Rezin, having pushed on to Elath, would not go home without some permanent gain.
7:
The verse gives Ahaz's fatal message to Assyria — "I am thy servant and thy son: come up, and save me" — and the witnesses, with one voice, read it as the self-abasement that would cost him more than the war. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the tone of the request: "which rise up against me" is better "which are assailing me," and behind it he hears, with Robertson Smith, a court whose "vain confidence" had given way to "equally unreasoning panic." They "saw only one way of escape — namely, to throw themselves upon the protection of Assyria," and could not see that the yoke they invited was heavier than the enemies they feared.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke fills out the words of the message. To call himself Tiglath-pileser's "servant and son" was to say, "I will obey thee in all, and become tributary to thee; only help me against Syria and Israel" — a formula of vassalage offered by a king of David's line to a heathen overlord. John Wesley presses the theology the deed betrays, echoing Isaiah's own reproach: "But was it because there was no God in Israel that he sent to the Assyrian for help?" And he draws the verdict that governs the whole episode: "The sin itself was its own punishment; for though it served his present turn, yet he made but an ill bargain, seeing he not only impoverished himself, but enslaved both himself and his people."

The reformed John Gill passes over the message quickly, only identifying the king addressed; but the point on which the witnesses converge is unmistakable, and it answers back to Matthew Henry's charge on the preceding verses. Ahaz had a God even then protecting him, a prophet even then sent to steady him, and a promise even then held out; had he humbled himself to the Lord as abjectly as he now humbled himself to Assyria, he might have been delivered on far easier terms — saving his treasure and parting only with his sins. Instead, seeking help where there was no help, he purchased a brief reprieve at the price of servitude, and the words "thy servant and thy son" become the epitaph of a faith surrendered to fear. The rescue he bought would prove, in the end, a harder master than the kings he dreaded.
8:
The price of rescue is now paid, and the witnesses trace both its source and its consequence. The reformed John Gill notes where the treasure came from: it was the silver and gold that Uzziah and Jotham had laid up in the house of the Lord, for all that had been stored in Amaziah's day was long since carried off by Jehoash of Israel (2 Kings 14:14). The grammatical-historical commentators supply the outcome. Keil and Delitzsch record that Tiglath-pileser marched on Damascus, took it, slew Rezin, and led its people captive to Kir, exactly as Amos had foretold (Amos 1:3–5) — Kir being, they judge, a district by the river Kur that flows from Armenia toward the Caspian, though the length of that river forbids precision.

Charles Ellicott, drawing again on Robertson Smith, exposes the transaction for what it was: Ahaz "was well aware that the only conditions on which protection would be vouchsafed were acceptance of the Assyrian suzerainty with the payment of a huge tribute," and the embassy went "laden with all the treasures of the palace and the Temple." Its object "perfectly fell in with the schemes of the great king": Damascus fell, and Gilead and Galilee suffered the same captivity (2 Kings 15:29). Ellicott then anchors the whole episode in the monuments — Tiglath-pileser's own inscription lists the tribute of "Jahuhazi (Jehoahaz, i.e., Ahaz) of Judah" among the princes of Ammon, Moab, Ascalon, Edom, and Gaza, fixing the year of Ahaz's submission at 734 B.C.

The witnesses do not divide over the facts, and their shared judgment is the one Matthew Henry had already pronounced on the bargain: "both church and state must be squeezed and exhausted" to gratify the new patron. That the rescue-money should be robbed from "the house of the Lord" is the sting of it — a king who would not trust God plundered God's own treasury to buy the help of a heathen, and (as the next verses will show) reaped from that alliance not deliverance but distress. Ahaz, Gill implies, gave away the accumulated wealth of two godly reigns to secure a servitude that no tribute could ever buy back.
9:
The verse records Assyria's compliance — Damascus taken, its people deported, Rezin slain — and the witnesses weigh both the archaeology and the irony of the "help." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the record from the monuments: the inscriptions show that Damascus "stood a two years' siege" (733–732 B.C.), and Sir H. Rawlinson found on a tablet of Tiglath-pileser's the very fact of Rezin's death, the great king boasting that he impaled the city's chief officers alive. He adds the learned caution about Kir, "the aboriginal home of the Arameans" (Amos 9:7; Isaiah 22:6): though often placed by the river Cyrus, both the philology and the reach of the Assyrian empire suggest rather a part of Mesopotamia.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke, however, will not let the "success" pass as success. He sets against it the Chronicler's verdict that Tiglath-pileser "distressed him, but strengthened him not" (2 Chronicles 28:20): though the Assyrian came, took Damascus, and slew Rezin, "he did not help Ahaz against the Philistines, nor did he lend him any forces against Israel," and he emptied the royal and sacred treasuries for little return. Clarke recalls how near Pekah had come to ruining Judah — a hundred thousand valiant men slain in one day, two hundred thousand women and children carried toward Samaria, until at the prophet Oded's rebuke the captives were fed, clothed, and sent home (2 Chronicles 28:6; 2 Chronicles 28:8–15). "He helped him a little," Clarke concludes, "but distressed him on the whole."

The reformed John Gill marks only that the king of Assyria "complied with his request," but the shared point is Clarke's: the alliance bought a real but partial and costly victory, and its bill was ruinous. The northern threat was broken and Rezin was dead, yet Ahaz had mortgaged the independence of Judah to gain it, and the very deliverance drew the empire's shadow over Jerusalem. What looked like rescue was the first turn of a screw that Hezekiah's reign would feel and Judah's later kings would not escape. The witnesses concur: to buy safety from God's appointed chastisement by an unlawful alliance is to exchange a passing danger for a lasting one.
10:
At Damascus, doing homage to his overlord, Ahaz sees an altar that captivates him and sends its pattern home to be copied — and here the witnesses' indictment sharpens from idolatry to sacrilege. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene soberly. Keil and Delitzsch explain the visit: Ahaz went "to present his thanks and congratulations," and perhaps to forestall a visit from Tiglath-pileser to Jerusalem; there he saw an altar whose style so pleased him that he sent "a picture and model of it, according to all the workmanship thereof," to Urijah the priest. Charles Ellicott identifies that Urijah with the "faithful witness" whom Isaiah names (Isaiah 8:2), and, quoting Robertson Smith, fastens on the frivolity of the king: so little did Ahaz grasp the danger of his new servitude that "in a national crisis of the first magnitude he found no more pressing concern than the erection of a new altar in the Temple on a pattern brought from Damascus" — a trifling virtuoso whose sundial (2 Kings 20:11) and roof-altars to the host of heaven (2 Kings 23:12) were of a piece. The very words "fashion, pattern, workmanship," Ellicott adds, betray an interest artistic rather than religious.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke names the sin outright. This was "some idolatrous altar," and Ahaz meant it so, as the Chronicler proves: "he sacrificed unto the gods of Damascus... Because the gods of the kings of Syria help them, I will sacrifice to them, that they may help me" (2 Chronicles 28:23). For the altar Ahaz "had no Divine authority, and the compliance of Urijah was both mean and sinful." John Gill notes the sheer distance the king travelled to flatter his patron — some hundred and sixty miles to Damascus, on Bunting's reckoning.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the scene its fullest moral colour, and calls it "a bolder stroke than the worst of the kings had yet given to religion." Ahaz, "while viewing the rarities of the place, or rather while joining with them in their devotions," was charmed by an altar "curiously carved, and adorned with image-work," and thought Solomon's altar dull by comparison. "The pattern God showed to Moses in the mount," Henry observes with grief, was in his eyes not comparable "to this pattern sent from Damascus. The hearts of idolaters walked after their eyes... but the true worshippers worship the true God by faith." The witnesses agree without a dissenting voice: what began as the fancy of a superstitious tourist became the displacement of God's own appointed altar.
11:
The verse turns from the king's command to the priest's compliance: "Urijah the priest built an altar according to all that king Ahaz had sent from Damascus... against king Ahaz came." The witnesses fasten on the guilt of the priest, and the reformed John Gill states the charge precisely. Urijah built it "exactly according to the size, form, figure, and carved work" of the Syrian original — "though expressly contrary to the command of God," which had fixed "both the form and matter of the altar of God, with everything appertaining to it." As high priest he "could not be ignorant" of that law; his obedience to the king was therefore knowing treachery. Gill's verdict is unsparing: Urijah "was a timeserver, and sought to curry favour with his prince."

The puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the same act, presses harder still on what the priest betrayed. It was "a most base wicked thing" for a chief priest to make this altar "in compliance with an idolatrous prince." He may have flattered himself with a pretext — that by obliging the king he would "keep him to the temple at Jerusalem, and prevent his totally deserting it for the high places" — but the plea does not clear him. By it "he prostituted his authority and profaned the crown of his priesthood, making himself a servant to the lusts of men," and "there is not a greater disgrace to the ministry than obsequiousness to such wicked commands." He betrayed his trust: bound "to maintain and defend God's institutions, and to oppose all innovations," he instead helped set up an altar "to confront the altar which by divine appointment he was consecrated to minister at." Had he only connived, or delayed, or been frightened into it, it were less; but "so willingly to walk after the king's commandment, as if he were glad of the opportunity to oblige him," was inexcusable.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward without pausing on the blame: the altar was made and ready, and when the king came from Damascus he "approached to the altar and offered thereon," before proceeding to move the old bronze altar from its place. The witnesses do not differ here; they combine into a single, sobering picture — a priest who should have been the guardian of God's worship becoming the ready instrument of its corruption, so that, as Henry laments, "miserable is the case of great men when those that should reprove them for their sins strengthen and serve them in their sins."
12:
The king returns, sees the finished altar, and himself offers upon it — and the witnesses find in that single act a second usurpation layered on the first. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the textual work that yields the point. The Targum renders that "the king approached to the altar and offered thereon," but "all the other versions" read, "the king approached to the altar, and went up thereon" (compare 1 Kings 12:32–33). From this Ellicott draws a striking inference: "it thus appears that Ahaz, like Uzziah, personally exercised the priestly function of sacrifice." The king who had corrupted the altar now seizes the office, ascending it with his own hands — the very presumption for which Uzziah had been struck with leprosy (2 Chronicles 26:16–19), yet Ahaz repeats it without restraint.

The wesleyan John Wesley reads the act for its object as well as its actor: Ahaz "offered a sacrifice, and that not to God, but to the Syrian idols, to whom that altar was appropriated." The very design of the altar — copied from a heathen shrine and, on the Chronicler's testimony, dedicated to "the gods of Damascus" — determines whose worship it served.

The reformed John Gill notes only that the king "looked at it, and liked it, being exactly according to the pattern he had sent" — the artist's satisfaction of the "trifling virtuoso" Ellicott had described. The witnesses do not contend here, and their combined portrait is damning in its quietness: a Davidic king, home from doing homage to Assyria, mounts a Syrian altar in the courts of the Lord's house and there begins a worship that is neither in the appointed place, nor of the appointed form, nor — Wesley insists — to the appointed God. Every fence the law had set about the altar has now been broken, and by the king's own hand. What Uzziah had done in a moment of pride, and paid for in his flesh, Ahaz now does as settled policy, and the sanctuary offers no resistance. The king's evident satisfaction — Gill's note that he "liked it" — only deepens the pathos, for it shows a conscience so far gone that the profanation of the temple could please him as an artist is pleased with his work. Where the law had appointed the priest to sacrifice and the king to worship, Ahaz collapses the two offices into his own person and makes himself the arbiter of Israel's worship, answerable, he imagines, to none.
13:
The verse itemizes Ahaz's inaugural sacrifice on the new altar — burnt offering, meal offering, drink offering, and the blood of peace offerings — and the witnesses divide, gently, over whom he meant to worship. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the occasion as "the thank-offering of Ahaz for his late deliverance from deadly peril," and is candid that the surface of the narrative points one way: "from the present narrative it does not appear but that he offered it to Jehovah." He reconciles this with the Chronicler's charge of sacrificing to the gods of Damascus by referring that (2 Chronicles 28:23) to other sacrifices; for Ahaz, "like most of his contemporaries, thought the traditional worship of Jehovah not incompatible with the cultus of foreign deities" (compare 2 Kings 16:3–4). His was the syncretist's altar, meant to serve every power at once.

The wesleyan John Wesley reads it the other way, taking the peace offerings as belonging to the heathen rite: they were "for the heathens; and Ahaz, in imitation of them, offered the same sorts of offerings to their false gods which the Israelites did to the true." Where Ellicott sees a confused worship of Jehovah alongside idols, Wesley sees the forms of the true worship diverted wholesale to false gods.

The reformed John Gill stands between them on the matter of form, observing that "his burnt offering and his meat offering... went together according to the law of God, and was imitated by the Heathens" — the very outward pattern was one that the surrounding nations aped, so that the same act could face two ways at once. The difference among the witnesses is real but not fierce: it turns on whether Ahaz thought himself still worshipping the LORD, or had frankly transferred his devotion to Damascus. On the substance they are one. Whatever name the king pronounced, he had abandoned the altar God appointed for one of his own choosing, and the outward correctness of the rite — burnt, meal, drink, and peace offerings all duly presented — only made the corruption harder to detect and more perilous to the people who watched their king.
14:
The old bronze altar of burnt offering, made by Solomon and set "before the LORD," is now displaced, and the witnesses read its removal as the calculated heart of Ahaz's sacrilege. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott clarifies the maneuver. The bronze altar "used to stand before the Lord, in the middle of the court of the priests, and in front of the Temple proper." Urijah, it seems, had already pushed it forward nearer the sanctuary and set the new Syrian altar in its place; but Ahaz, "not satisfied with this arrangement, which appeared to confer a kind of precedence on the old altar, drew it back again, and fixed it on the north side of his new altar." The point is precedence: the God-appointed altar must not merely stand beside the intruder but yield the central place to it.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke sees a deliberate program behind every such change. Ahaz "seems to have intended to conform every thing in the Lord's house as much as possible to the idolatrous temples which he saw at Damascus, and to model the Divine worship in the same way — in a word, to honour and worship the gods of Syria, and not the God of heaven." All the alterations "were in contempt of the true God," and "thus he provoked to anger the LORD God of his fathers" (2 Chronicles 28:25). John Wesley presses the enormity of it in the strongest terms: the bronze altar had been "placed there by God's appointment," yet Ahaz "put God's altar out of its place and use" — "a bolder stroke than the very worst of kings had hitherto given to religion."

The reformed John Gill fixes the identity and station of the altar that was moved: it was the one "Solomon made," which "stood in the court by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the Lord," at the temple's eastern front. Matthew Henry, who has watched the whole sequence, reads it as the inevitable end of the contest between man's invention and God's institution: Ahaz's new altar, at first set modestly at the lower end of the court, "at length jostled out" the true one, for "those will soon come to make nothing of God that will not be content to make him their all." The witnesses do not divide; together they mark the moment the sign of God's appointed worship was pushed into a corner to make room for a king's imported novelty.
15:
Ahaz's final order transfers all Israel's worship to the new altar — the daily burnt and meal offerings, the king's and the people's sacrifices, all the blood — and reserves the old bronze altar with the enigmatic clause, "the brasen altar shall be for me to enquire by." Over that clause the witnesses openly divide. The grammatical-historical commentators lay out the options. Charles Ellicott records three: with Rashi, "for consulting God"; with Keil, "I will think about what to do with it"; or, more simply, "it shall be for me to look at," an ornamental duplicate (compare Psalms 27:4). Keil and Delitzsch themselves take it as a deferral — Ahaz stops the use of Solomon's altar "about which he would consider" — while noting that the new altar is called "the great altar" probably because it was somewhat larger than Solomon's.

The wesleyan John Wesley reads the clause as a claim of private privilege: the old altar "shall be reserved for my proper use, to enquire by; at which I may seek God, or enquire of his will, by sacrifices joined with prayer, when I shall see fit." But he sees through the pretence. "Having thrust it out from the use for which it was instituted, which was to sanctify the gifts offered upon it, he pretends to advance it above its institution" — the very habit of superstitious people. Wesley's proverb sums it: "But to overdo is to underdo. Our wisdom is, to do just what God has commanded."

The reformed John Gill fastens not on the clause but on the persons, and turns the verse against the priest. Ahaz "commanded Urijah the priest" — but Urijah "was not to be commanded by the king in matters of worship," being bound instead "to attend to the laws and institutions of God." Ellicott adds the ritual precision the order disturbs: "the morning burnt offering and the evening meat offering" does not mean there was no meal offering in the morning nor burnt offering at evening (Exodus 29:38–42; Numbers 28:3–8), for the evening burnt offering had to burn all the night through (Leviticus 6:9). Beneath their disagreement over one obscure phrase, the witnesses agree on the outrage it caps: the whole appointed worship of the LORD is now made to pass through a king's counterfeit, at a king's command, with a priest's consent.
16:
The single sentence of this verse — "Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded" — is the narrator's deliberate seal on the priest's compliance, and the witnesses read it as such. The reformed John Gill draws out its scope: Urijah obeyed "not only concerning the structure of the altar, but the sacrifices to be offered on it." Then he coins the verse's memorable verdict: "like king, like priest, both apostates and idolaters." The corruption of the throne has found a willing echo in the sanctuary, and the two are now bound in a common guilt.

The puritan Matthew Henry hears the repetition as pointed. This is the third time the narrative has stooped to record Urijah's obsequiousness, and Henry marks it as "the base compliance of the poor-spirited priest with the presumptuous usurpations of an ill-spirited king." From it he draws the lament that governs the whole episode: "Miserable is the case of great men when those that should reprove them for their sins strengthen and serve them in their sins." The office that God had set as a fence about his worship became, in Urijah's hands, the instrument for tearing that fence down; a bolder priest might have stayed the king, but a servile one only sped his ruin.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the verse as the pivot to the next stage of desecration. Having noted that Urijah "did according to all that king Ahaz commanded," they pass at once to what the king did next with his own hands — cutting off the ornamented panels of Solomon's bronze stands, lifting the lavers from them, and taking down the great bronze sea from the twelve oxen that bore it, to set it on a pavement of stones. The witnesses do not divide here; the verse stands as a hinge and a judgment together. It closes the tale of the altar with the sorrowful proverb "like king, like priest," and it opens the tale of the plundered furniture — a reminder that when the guardians of worship will not resist an idolatrous prince, there is nothing left in the house of God that is safe.
17:
Ahaz now lays hands on the temple's sacred vessels, and the witnesses read the vandalism as an assault on the very means of priestly purity. The reformed John Gill describes exactly what was destroyed. In the temple stood ten lavers "for the priests to wash in," set upon bases of brass; and about those bases ran borders bearing "figures of various creatures, lions, oxen, and cherubim" (1 Kings 7:28–29). These carved borders Ahaz "cut off, either to deface them, in contempt of them, or to convert the brass to other uses," and he took the lavers down from their stands — perhaps meaning to melt the metal, perhaps only to spoil the work.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the motive as spite against the priesthood's holiness. Ahaz "defaced the bases on which the lavers were set, and took down the molten sea"; and since "these the priests used for washing, against them therefore he seems to have had a particular spite." Henry lifts the act into a principle: "It is one of the greatest prejudices that can be done to religion to obstruct the purifying of the priests, the Lord's ministers." To cripple the cleansing of those who serve at the altar is to strike at worship near its root, cutting off the symbol of that sanctity without which none dared approach the holy things.

The witnesses do not divide over so plain a desecration; instead they let it stand within the descending pattern of the reign. Where the earlier verses had corrupted the altar and displaced it, this verse begins the stripping of the house itself — the lavers robbed of their ornamented stands, the great bronze sea lifted from the twelve oxen that had borne it since Solomon's day and set down on a bare "pavement of stones." Gill and Keil and Delitzsch alike note that the reason is left unstated; the narrative simply records the mutilation and leaves the reader to feel its meanness. A king who had once thought no altar fine enough but a Syrian one now hacks the carved brass from God's furniture, whether from contempt or from greed — and either way the house that David longed for and Solomon built is being dismantled piece by piece by a son of their own line.
18:
The last of Ahaz's changes is the most obscure — he "turned from the house of the LORD" both "the covert for the sabbath" and "the king's entry without," and did it "for the king of Assyria." The witnesses freely confess the difficulty. The wesleyan John Wesley admits the "covert" is a thing "the form and use whereof is now unknown," and offers the leading guesses: a building where the priests lodged when their weekly course ended, or where the temple guard kept station, or "that under which the king used to sit to hear God's word, and see the sacrifices," called "of the sabbath" because the king used it chiefly on the weekly sabbath and other solemn days. Adam Clarke multiplies the conjectures — a canopy for the people's shelter, or for the king's repose — and reports Jarchi's view that it was "a sort of covert way that the kings of Judah had to the temple," which Ahaz removed "lest the king of Assyria, going by that way and seeing the sacred vessels, should covet them."

The grammatical-historical commentators settle on the likeliest sense while guarding it. Charles Ellicott takes it as "the covered hall set apart for the use of the king and his attendants when he visited the Temple on holy days," and the "king's entry without" as the outer gate by which the king passed into the inner court (Ezekiel 46:1–2). Keil and Delitzsch agree it was a covered stand in the court for the king's use on sabbaths and feasts, and firmly reject Ewald's and Thenius's guess that "turned" means "robbed of their ornaments" as "quite arbitrary."

On why Ahaz did it "for the king of Assyria" the witnesses lean one way. Ellicott reads the phrase as "from fear of" — "Ahaz durst not appear before Tiglath without a present," and perhaps anticipated a visit from the great king. The reformed John Gill catches the spiritual meaning under the political: whatever the fabric was, Ahaz took it away "for the king of Assyria, or to signify that he should not frequent the place any more." That, for Matthew Henry, is the sting of the verse: a king who turns the ready passage between his palace and the house of God "another way, to please his neighbours," is "going down the hill apace towards his ruin." The witnesses do not resolve the archaeology, but they agree on the direction of the heart — every alteration bends the temple further toward Assyria and further from the God whose house it was.
19:
The reign closes with the historian's familiar formula — "the rest of the acts of Ahaz which he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?" — and the witnesses, few and agreed, read it for what it discloses about the sources and the shape of the record. The reformed John Gill explains the reference: "some others are written in the canonical book of Chronicles," and were, "it is highly probable, in the annals of the kings of Judah, now lost." The verse thus points beyond itself to a fuller royal chronicle no longer extant, of which the inspired history has preserved only what the reader most needs — and in Ahaz's case that is a portrait almost wholly of apostasy, the Chronicler filling in still darker strokes (2 Chronicles 28:22–25).

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the wording and its sequel. Charles Ellicott notes a small variant: some manuscripts, with the LXX, Syriac, and Arabic, carry the usual fuller formula, "and all which he did," rounding off the reign in the accustomed way. Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the formal "conclusion of the reign of Ahaz," and read it together with the notice of his death that follows, marking already the detail the Chronicler adds — that he "was buried in the city of David, but not in the sepulchres of the kings" (2 Chronicles 28:27). The formula of closure, in their hands, is no bare full-stop but the doorway to that verdict of dishonour.

Here the witnesses do not debate; the verse offers nothing to divide over, and they let its very brevity speak. The stock phrase that had crowned so many reigns falls, in Ahaz's, upon a life the narrator has been at pains to expose — the passing through the fire, the plundered treasury, the imported altar, the mutilated vessels, the closed approach to the house of God. That such a reign should be dismissed with the same formula as any other is itself a kind of judgment: the "rest of his acts" are remitted to a lost book, while what Scripture keeps is the record of his sin and its ruin. The reader is left waiting only for the turn of v.20, where the throne of this worst of Judah's kings passes, by a mercy he did not earn, to the reforming hand of Hezekiah his son.
20:
The chapter ends as it must — "Ahaz slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David: and Hezekiah his son reigned in his stead" — and the witnesses fasten on the one detail that turns a common formula into a verdict. The reformed John Gill notes that Ahaz was buried "in the city of David," yet "not in the sepulchres of the kings of Israel, as David and Solomon, he being such a wicked prince" — a distinction the Chronicler makes explicit (2 Chronicles 28:27). Even in death the dishonour of his reign clings to him; he lies within David's city but shut out from David's royal tombs.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke turns the burial into an epitaph on the whole life. That Ahaz was "not buried in the sepulchres of the kings" was "undoubtedly intended as a mark of degradation," for "his reign was disastrous and impious; and it was disastrous because it was impious." Clarke tallies the two: Ahaz "had been a scourge, not a blessing, to his people," making illegal alterations in the temple and worship and polluting "all the cities of Judah with idolatry." On the whole, he judges, "a worse king than himself had not as yet sat on the Jewish throne." And yet the aggravation was that he had every advantage — "for counsellor one of the greatest men ever produced in the Jewish nation, Isaiah the prophet," and a God who "condescended to interpose especially for him" against Rezin and Pekah, both cut off just as the prophet foretold. "But he would not lay it to heart," and so "the wrath of God fell heavily upon him." Clarke's closing words are stern: "He had sufficient warning, and was without excuse. He would sin, and therefore he must suffer."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the succession plainly — "Hezekiah his son reigned in his stead" — and in that bare notice the witnesses find their one gleam of hope. The puritan Matthew Henry, looking back over the reign, marks the mercy of it: Ahaz died "in the midst of his days, at thirty-six," and left the kingdom "to a better man, Hezekiah his son, who proved as much a friend to the temple as he had been an enemy to it." Henry adds the arresting thought that this very son Ahaz "had perhaps made to pass through the fire," dedicating him to Moloch, yet "God, by his grace, snatched him as a brand out of the burning." The witnesses do not divide; together they close the account of Judah's worst king with the note that his sin was its own punishment, and that the God he despised had already prepared a deliverer from within his own house.
